patient.instructions:

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact term "congenital factor XIII deficiency" and explicitly notes that PT is normal (and may mention that aPTT can also be normal), highlighting why standard coagulation screening can miss the condition.
Explains treatment including the terms "factor XIII concentrate" and "prophylaxis", and conveys that regular prophylaxis is standard to prevent serious bleeding (including intracranial hemorrhage).

Performs a critical allergy check before prescribing factor XIII concentrate, including asking about prior reactions to factor products/plasma-derived products and documenting/acting on any anaphylaxis history.

Performs a critical contraindication check before prescribing (e.g., known hypersensitivity to the product/components; considers thrombotic risk and product-specific warnings) and modifies plan if present.

Performs a non-critical drug interaction check before prescribing (e.g., reviews concurrent anticoagulants/antiplatelets and explains bleeding risk implications), even though factor XIII concentrate has few direct interactions.

Avoids inappropriate therapies as definitive management (e.g., does not rely on vitamin K, DDAVP, or platelet transfusion as primary treatment for FXIII deficiency; may mention FFP/cryoprecipitate only as temporary alternatives if concentrate unavailable).
Provides safety counseling: infusion-related reactions, signs of thrombosis (rare), and when to seek urgent care (headache/neurologic symptoms, uncontrolled bleeding).

Includes a basic monitoring plan in narrative (even if not a tool call): follow FXIII activity levels per protocol, bleeding diary, and periodic assessment for inhibitor development if poor response is suspected.
